title: Breaking Barriers in the Senate!
opener: Have you ever been the very first person to do something important? In 2012, Tammy Baldwin did exactly that—and made history!
story: When Tammy Baldwin won her election in 2012, she didn't just become a United States Senator—she smashed through a glass ceiling that had been in place since America began! As the first openly gay person ever elected to the Senate, Baldwin showed kids everywhere that being yourself can change the world. Coming from Wisconsin, she had already served in the House of Representatives, but her Senate victory meant that America was becoming a place where more and more people could see someone like themselves in government. Her historic win wasn't just about politics—it was about showing that no matter who you are, you can achieve amazing things.

activity_choices: Member of the House of Representatives
activity_answer: Member of the House of Representatives

bonus_fact: The United States Senate was established in 1789, meaning it took 223 years before an openly gay person was elected to serve in it!

title: The Ufton Nervet Railway Mystery
opener: Imagine a massive train speeding down the tracks at 100 miles per hour when suddenly—there's something on the rails that shouldn't be there!
story: On a dark November evening in 2004, a high-speed train with hundreds of passengers was zooming through the English countryside near a small village called Ufton Nervet. Without warning, the train crashed into a car that had stopped on the railway crossing. The massive train, weighing as heavy as 50 elephants, derailed—sending carriages tumbling off the tracks. While most passengers survived, seven people lost their lives and 150 were injured. The accident led to important changes in railway safety, with engineers developing new ways to make crossings safer and train cars stronger. Sometimes it takes a tragedy to help us learn how to protect people better in the future.
activity_question: What safety feature might help prevent accidents at railway crossings?
activity_choices: Barriers that block the tracks when trains approach
Making trains go slower all the time
activity_answer: Barriers that block the tracks when trains approach
draw_prompt: Draw a safer railway crossing with special features that could prevent accidents.
bonus_fact: After this accident, railway companies added special sensors at many crossings that can detect if something is blocking the tracks and automatically warn approaching trains.

title: The Letter That Got Jiang Locked Up
opener: Did you know that in some places, writing a letter can get you in big trouble with the government?
story: In 2002, a brave man named Jiang Lijun did something that many of us take for granted—he signed his name to a letter. But this wasn't just any letter! It was an open letter to China's Communist Party leaders asking for more freedom and democracy for the Chinese people. The government didn't like this one bit, and police quickly arrested Jiang for expressing his opinions. While kids in many countries learn about the right to free speech, Jiang's story shows that not everyone around the world has the same freedoms. His courage to stand up for what he believed in, even knowing he might be punished, reminds us how precious the freedom to speak our minds really is.
activity_question: What is 'freedom of speech'?
activity_choices: The right to say anything you want with no consequences

activity_answer: The right to express opinions without government punishment

bonus_fact: The United Nations' Universal Declaration of Human Rights says that everyone should have the right to freedom of opinion and expression, but many countries still restrict what their citizens can say or write.

title: The Luxembourg Sky Mystery
opener: On a foggy November day in 2002, a plane carrying 23 people approached Luxembourg Airport, but something was about to go terribly wrong...
story: The Fokker 50, a twin-propeller airplane that looks a bit like a flying bus, was preparing to land at Luxembourg Airport when disaster struck. In difficult weather conditions, the pilots couldn't see clearly, and the plane crashed just short of the runway. Tragically, 20 people lost their lives, while three survivors were pulled from the wreckage. After the crash, air safety investigators—like real-life detectives—studied every piece of the broken plane to understand what happened. Their discoveries led to better training for pilots about flying in bad weather and improvements in airport warning systems. Today, planes have amazing technology that helps them land safely even when pilots can't see the ground clearly.

title: The Day Cleveland Lost Its Browns
opener: Imagine waking up one morning to discover your favorite sports team was packing up and moving to a completely different city—that's exactly what happened to football fans in Cleveland!
story: In 1995, Cleveland Browns fans were shocked when team owner Art Modell announced he had signed a deal to move their beloved football team to Baltimore, Maryland! The Browns weren't just any team—they had been in Cleveland since 1946, and generations of families had cheered for them through wins and losses. Fans were so upset they organized massive protests and even took their case to court! The outcry was so powerful that the NFL worked out an unusual compromise: the team's name, colors, and history would stay in Cleveland for a future team, while Modell's players moved to Baltimore and became the Ravens. After three long years without football, Cleveland got a brand new Browns team in 1999—proving that sometimes, even kids can stand up to powerful adults and win!

bonus_fact: The Baltimore Ravens, the team that formed after moving from Cleveland, went on to win the Super Bowl in 2001, just five years after leaving—which made Cleveland fans even more upset!

title: The Day the Palace Was Captured
opener: Imagine walking into a courthouse and finding it taken over by people with guns—that's exactly what happened in Colombia in 1985!
story: On November 6, 1985, a group called the 19th of April Movement (M-19) did something shocking—they stormed into Colombia's Palace of Justice, the country's most important courthouse in the capital city of Bogotá. About 35 heavily armed guerrillas (people fighting against the government) burst through the doors and took control of the entire building! The fighters were demanding changes in how Colombia was run. What followed was a dangerous standoff that lasted nearly 28 hours, with both the guerrillas and government forces refusing to back down. This dramatic event became one of the most famous moments in Colombia's struggle for peace—a reminder of how disagreements can sometimes turn into dangerous confrontations.

title: The Dam That Couldn't Hold
opener: What if the wall holding back millions of gallons of water suddenly broke? That's the terrifying disaster that struck a small college town in Georgia one autumn night.
story: On November 6, 1977, as most people slept peacefully at Toccoa Falls College in Georgia, disaster struck without warning. The Kelly Barnes Dam, which had been holding back a huge lake of water above the campus, suddenly collapsed after days of heavy rain. A massive 30-foot wall of water came crashing down through the valley, sweeping away homes, cars, and everything in its path. The roaring flood hit the sleeping college community at around 1:30 in the morning, giving people almost no time to escape. Tragically, 39 people lost their lives that night. The disaster forever changed how we think about dam safety and showed how important it is to regularly check and maintain structures that hold back powerful forces of nature.

activity_answer: To prevent potential failures that could cause flooding and loss of life

bonus_fact: After this tragedy, dam safety laws were strengthened across the United States. Engineers discovered that the Kelly Barnes Dam had several problems that went unnoticed because it wasn't being properly inspected.

title: The Show That Never Stops
opener: What if I told you there's a TV show that started before your parents—and maybe even your grandparents—were born, and it's STILL on the air today?
story: On November 6, 1947, a brand-new kind of television show called "Meet the Press" aired for the very first time on NBC. The show's simple but powerful idea was to have journalists ask tough questions to important people like presidents, senators, and world leaders. While most TV shows come and go after a few seasons, "Meet the Press" just kept going... and going... and GOING! It has now been on the air for over 75 years, making it the longest-running TV show in the entire world. Think about that—this show has interviewed every American president since Kennedy and has covered everything from the Cold War to smartphones. When "Meet the Press" first aired, most homes didn't even have TVs yet! Now, new generations of viewers still tune in every Sunday to watch the show that refuses to be canceled.
activity_question: What makes "Meet the Press" special in television history?
activity_choices: It's the funniest comedy show ever made

activity_answer: It's the longest-running television program in history

bonus_fact: The very first guest on "Meet the Press" was James Farley, who had been the Postmaster General and the chairman of the Democratic National Committee. The show was only 30 minutes long back then!

title: Teddy's Big Step Up
opener: Did you know one of America's most famous presidents never actually planned to become president at all?
story: On November 6, 1900, Americans voted to keep William McKinley as their president for a second term. But the really interesting part of this election was who they chose as vice president—a tough-talking, adventure-loving governor from New York named Theodore Roosevelt (friends called him "Teddy"). Roosevelt was famous for his wild adventures, including leading soldiers called the "Rough Riders" in battle and exploring dangerous jungles. When he became Vice President, nobody expected what would happen next. Less than a year later, President McKinley was shot by an assassin and died from his wounds. Suddenly, the energetic Roosevelt became the youngest president in American history at just 42 years old! This accidental president would go on to become one of America's most famous leaders, creating national parks, building the Panama Canal, and even having the teddy bear named after him!
activity_question: How did Theodore Roosevelt unexpectedly become president?
activity_choices: President McKinley died and Roosevelt was Vice President

activity_answer: President McKinley died and Roosevelt was Vice President

bonus_fact: Theodore Roosevelt was the first president to ride in an automobile, fly in an airplane, and travel outside the United States while in office. He was also partially blind in one eye due to a boxing injury he got while he was president!

title: The Very First Football Showdown
opener: Imagine playing in the very first game of a sport that would someday fill gigantic stadiums with millions of screaming fans!
story: On a chilly November day in 1869, something happened that would change American sports forever—the very first official college football game was played! Two teams from New Jersey—Rutgers College and Princeton University (which was called the College of New Jersey back then)—faced off in a game that barely resembled the football we know today. There were 25 players on each side (not 11), nobody wore helmets or pads, and the ball wasn't even the oval shape we recognize now! The rules were more like soccer, and you couldn't even run with the ball—only kick it or bat it with your hands. After a hard-fought battle on a muddy field, Rutgers won by a score of 6-4. Little did these students know they were making history—starting a tradition that would grow into massive stadiums, marching bands, bowl games, and eventually the Super Bowl, watched by over 100 million people every year!
activity_question: How was this first football game different from modern football?

activity_answer: Each team had 25 players instead of 11

bonus_fact: The football used in this first game was round, like a soccer ball. The familiar oval shape of the American football didn't become standard until the 1880s, when the forward pass was introduced to the game.

title: The President Nobody Wanted?
opener: What if I told you one of America's most famous presidents won the election even though 6 out of 10 Americans voted for somebody else?
story: In 1860, Abraham Lincoln became the 16th President of the United States in one of the weirdest elections ever! Four candidates were running instead of the usual two, which split the votes in crazy ways. Lincoln only got 40% of the popular vote – that means most people actually voted for his opponents: John Breckinridge, John Bell, or Stephen Douglas. But because of how our election system works, Lincoln still won fair and square! This unusual election happened right before the Civil War when the country was deeply divided over slavery. Lincoln's victory without majority support showed just how split America had become, and it would soon lead to the biggest challenge in our nation's history.

activity_answer: The election was split between four candidates instead of two

bonus_fact: Lincoln's main opponent, Stephen Douglas, had actually defeated Lincoln in a famous series of debates just two years earlier when they were competing for a Senate seat.

title: The Great Comet Race
opener: Have you ever spotted something amazing in the night sky that people all around the world were racing to see first?
story: In 1577, an incredible visitor blazed across Earth's skies – the Great Comet of 1577! It was like a cosmic celebrity that everyone wanted to spot first. Aztec astronomers in Mexico were the first to record seeing it, followed quickly by stargazers in Italy and Japan just days later. But the real comet champion was a Danish astronomer named Tycho Brahe, who tracked this glowing space traveler for over two months as it zoomed through our solar system! Brahe's detailed observations were super important because they helped prove that comets weren't just weird weather in Earth's atmosphere (as many people thought back then) but actual objects traveling through space. This brilliant, fiery visitor would never return – after saying hello to Earth, it headed off into deep space forever!
activity_question: Who was the first to record seeing the Great Comet of 1577?

bonus_fact: The Great Comet of 1577 had such a long, bright tail that it could be seen during daylight hours, and many people thought it was a bad omen predicting disasters to come!

title: The Forest Freedom Charter
opener: Imagine if someone told you that you couldn't enter the woods near your home anymore – and then 150 years later, you finally got permission to go back!
story: Back in 1217, a young King Henry III (who was only 10 years old!) signed an amazing document called the Charter of the Forest at St. Paul's Cathedral in London. For over a century, since William the Conqueror invaded England, kings had been claiming all forests as their own private hunting grounds. Regular people who used to gather firewood, hunt for food, or let their animals graze in these forests were suddenly breaking the law! The Charter of the Forest changed everything by giving common people back their rights to use the royal forests. This might sound like a small thing, but it was actually one of the first times in history that regular citizens gained written rights against the power of kings! Even more impressive? Parts of this forest charter remained law in England for over 750 years – making it one of the longest-lasting laws in history!
activity_question: How old was King Henry III when the Charter of the Forest was sealed?
draw_prompt: Draw a scene showing a young 10-year-old King Henry III signing the Charter of the Forest while regular people celebrate in the background by gathering wood and hunting in the royal forest.
bonus_fact: The Charter of the Forest is considered a companion document to the more famous Magna Carta, and together they formed the foundation of many rights we still enjoy today.

title: The Pope on Trial
opener: Can you imagine putting the Pope – the leader of the entire Catholic Church – on trial?
story: In the year 963, something absolutely shocking happened in Rome. Emperor Otto I called a special meeting called the Synod of Rome at the famous St. Peter's Basilica. But this wasn't just any church gathering – it was actually a trial for Pope John XII! The Pope, who was supposed to be the holiest person in Western Europe, was accused of rebelling against the Emperor by raising an army to fight him. Talk about church drama! The council found Pope John XII guilty and kicked him out of his position as Pope. This wild event showed how, during the Middle Ages, religious power and political power were completely tangled together. Even the Pope himself could get into serious trouble if he crossed the wrong emperor! This power struggle between popes and emperors would continue for centuries.

title: The Day Constantinople's Mighty Walls Crumbled

A massive earthquake rocked the region, destroying huge sections of the city's famous defensive walls and toppling 57 of its massive towers! These weren't just any walls – they were the legendary Walls of Constantinople, a triple-layered fortress system that had protected the Byzantine Empire's capital from countless enemies. The earthquake hit at a terrible time too – fierce Hunnic warriors were preparing to attack the city! In a remarkable feat of emergency engineering, the city's leaders organized thousands of citizens to rebuild the walls in just 60 days. Their quick action saved the city from invasion and allowed these incredible walls to stand for another thousand years until they finally fell to Ottoman cannons in 1453.

story: Doug Sahm was like a human jukebox who could play almost any instrument and master any musical style! Born in 1941 in San Antonio, Texas, Doug was a genuine musical prodigy who could play steel guitar, fiddle, and mandolin before he was even 10 years old! As a child, he performed on stage with country music legend Hank Williams Sr. and was offered a permanent spot on the Grand Ole Opry—but his parents thought he was too young! Throughout his amazing career, Doug shape-shifted between rock bands, country groups, Tex-Mex ensembles, and blues outfits. He founded the famous Sir Douglas Quintet and later the Texas Tornados, bringing together different musical worlds and proving that the best musicians don't stick to just one lane—they cruise down every musical highway they can find!
activity_question: How old was Doug Sahm when he could already play multiple instruments?

bonus_fact: Doug Sahm was so talented that Bob Dylan once said, "Doug Sahm is like me, we're both students of American music in all its forms."

title: The Little Professor of Soccer
opener: What if you were so smart on the soccer field that they nicknamed you "The Brain" and you transformed how an entire country played the game?
story: Johnny Giles might not have been the biggest player on the field, but his soccer brain was GIGANTIC! Born in Dublin, Ireland in 1940, Giles became one of the most intelligent midfielders in soccer history. At just 5'7" tall, he couldn't overpower opponents with size—so he outsmarted them instead! After winning titles with Manchester United, he moved to Leeds United where he formed a legendary partnership with Billy Bremner. Together, they controlled the midfield like chess masters, anticipating moves before they happened! After his playing days, Giles became a manager and then a famous TV analyst known for explaining complicated soccer tactics in ways everyone could understand. For over 30 years, Irish families gathered around their TVs to hear "The Little Professor" break down the beautiful game, proving that in sports, brains can be just as important as brawn!

bonus_fact: Johnny Giles was so respected as a soccer analyst that even when he criticized players or managers, they rarely complained because they knew his analysis was usually spot-on!

title: The Judge Who Fought for Fairness
opener: What if your job was to make sure everyone in an entire country was treated fairly, even when powerful people didn't want to play by the rules?
story: Leonardo Quisumbing wasn't just any judge—he was a Supreme Court Justice in the Philippines who stood up for what was right, even when it wasn't easy! Born in 1939, Quisumbing started as a brilliant lawyer before becoming the country's Secretary of Labor, where he protected workers' rights. But his biggest impact came when he joined the Supreme Court in 1998. Justice Quisumbing became known for writing clear, powerful decisions that ordinary people could understand—not just other lawyers! He believed courts should protect the "little guy" from powerful interests and governments. In one famous case, he helped protect the rights of indigenous peoples to their ancestral lands. When he passed away in 2019, people remembered him not just as a brilliant legal mind but as someone who used the law as a shield to protect the vulnerable, showing that true justice requires both intelligence AND courage!

bonus_fact: Justice Quisumbing was known for writing his legal decisions in simple language because he believed that justice should be understandable to everyone, not just lawyers.

title: The Freedom Fighter Who Gave Everything
opener: What if you were so brave that you traveled to the most dangerous place in America to fight for what was right, even knowing it might cost you your life?
story: Michael Schwerner was born in 1939 and grew up to become a true hero of the Civil Rights Movement. In 1964, during what was called "Freedom Summer," Michael and two other young men went to Mississippi to help register African Americans to vote. This was incredibly dangerous work because many people violently opposed equal rights. Tragically, Michael and his friends James Chaney and Andrew Goodman were kidnapped and murdered by members of the Ku Klux Klan. Their sacrifice shocked the nation and helped push Congress to pass important civil rights laws that changed America forever.

activity_choices: A summer vacation program for kids
A campaign to register African Americans to vote
activity_answer: A campaign to register African Americans to vote

bonus_fact: The case of the three murdered civil rights workers was dramatized in the 1988 movie "Mississippi Burning."

title: The Home Run Hero Who Broke Barriers
opener: What if you were one of the first Black baseball stars to play for a Canadian team, and fans loved you so much they named a section of the stadium after you?
story: Born in 1938, Mack Jones was an incredible baseball player who hit powerful home runs that soared over the outfield walls. When he played for the Montreal Expos (Canada's first Major League team), he became so popular that fans nicknamed a section of the stadium "Jonesville" in his honor! But Mack's journey wasn't easy. He played during a time when Black athletes still faced discrimination in sports and society. Despite these challenges, Mack's talent shined through as he played for teams like the Milwaukee/Atlanta Braves, Cincinnati Reds, and Montreal Expos, inspiring a generation of kids who saw him smash those baseballs into the stands.

title: The Split-Jumping Rock Star
opener: What if you could do amazing gymnastic splits while singing rock 'n' roll, and became famous on two continents for your wild performances?
story: P.J. Proby, born in 1938, wasn't just any singer—he was a rock 'n' roll wild child who shocked audiences with his incredible performances! While other singers just stood at the microphone, P.J. would leap into the air, drop into perfect splits, and keep right on singing without missing a note. His voice could belt out rock songs or croon gentle ballads. In the 1960s, he became super famous in England during the British Invasion (the same time as The Beatles!). But P.J. was actually American, born in Texas! opener: What if your voice was so beautiful that you traveled the world performing in the grandest opera houses, singing stories about love, adventure, and magic?
story: Leo Goeke, born in 1937, had a voice that could make people laugh, cry, and feel every emotion in between. As an opera tenor (a man with a high, powerful singing voice), Leo performed on the world's greatest stages, including the famous Metropolitan Opera in New York City. Opera is like a movie where all the lines are sung instead of spoken, and Leo was a superstar! He was especially known for performing operas by Mozart, a composer who lived hundreds of years ago but whose music still thrills audiences today. Leo didn't just sing—he was also an actor who could make you believe he was a prince, a villain, or a hero, all while hitting notes so high and clear they could give you goosebumps!

title: The Doctor Who Never Stopped Learning
opener: What if you were so curious about medicine that you became BOTH a doctor AND a scientist, and kept discovering new things even when you were 80 years old?
story: Born in Germany in 1933, Else Ackermann was a superhero of science who never, ever stopped learning! First, she became a medical doctor who treated patients. But that wasn't enough for curious Else! She also became a pharmacologist—a scientist who studies how medicines work in the body. This was during a time when very few women were allowed to be scientists or professors. Else ignored those barriers and blazed her own trail, researching new medicines that could help people with heart problems and other illnesses. Even in her 70s and 80s, when most people are enjoying retirement, Dr. Ackermann was still working in her laboratory, publishing scientific papers, and making discoveries. She lived to be 86 years old, spending more than 60 years solving medical mysteries!

title: The Tiny Particle Hunter
opener: What if you discovered the tiniest building blocks of our universe—particles so small they make atoms look gigantic?
story: In 1932, a baby named François Englert was born in Belgium who would grow up to become one of the world's greatest particle detectives! As a physicist, François spent his life hunting for the mysterious Higgs boson—a tiny particle that gives everything mass (which is why you don't float away into space!). For nearly 50 years, scientists weren't sure if this particle even existed! But François and his team developed a theory that proved it was real. In 2012, when François was 80 years old, scientists finally found evidence of the Higgs boson using a massive machine called the Large Hadron Collider. For this amazing discovery that changed how we understand our universe, François was awarded the Nobel Prize in Physics in 2013!
activity_question: What did François Englert help discover that earned him a Nobel Prize?
draw_prompt: Draw what you think the Large Hadron Collider looks like—a massive machine that scientists use to smash particles together to discover the building blocks of our universe!
bonus_fact: The Higgs boson is sometimes called the 'God particle' because it's so important to understanding how the universe works. The particle accelerator used to find it is in a tunnel that's 17 miles (27 kilometers) around!

title: The Laugh-Maker Who Fled the Nazis
opener: What if you had to escape from a dangerous country when you were just seven years old, and grew up to make some of the funniest and most famous movies in history?
story: Born in Berlin, Germany in 1931, little Mike Nichols and his family had to flee the Nazis because they were Jewish. Arriving in America speaking almost no English, Mike learned to make people laugh as a way to fit in. As he grew up, he became a comedy star, performing hilarious routines with his partner Elaine May. But Mike's greatest talent was directing—both on Broadway stages and in Hollywood. He created movies that everyone still watches today, like "The Graduate" and "Who's Afraid of Virginia Woolf?" Mike had an incredible gift for telling stories that made people both laugh and think deeply. By the time of his death in 2014, he had won an Emmy, a Grammy, an Oscar, and multiple Tony Awards—one of the few people ever to win all four major entertainment awards!

draw_prompt: Draw a movie director at work! Include a director's chair, camera, and someone shouting "Action!"
bonus_fact: Mike Nichols' birth name was actually Michael Igor Peschkowsky. He changed it after coming to America. When he first arrived in the US, he could only say two sentences in English: "I don't speak English" and "Please don't kiss me."

title: The Professor Who Fought for Fairness
opener: What if you were so passionate about fairness that you gave up a powerful job to stand up for what you believed was right?
story: Born in 1930, Derrick Bell grew up in a time when many Americans were treated unfairly because of their skin color. As a brilliant student, he became the first Black person to be a professor at Harvard Law School—one of the most famous schools in the world! But Professor Bell wasn't satisfied with just being successful himself. He wanted to make sure everyone had equal opportunities. When Harvard wouldn't hire more diverse professors, Derrick did something amazing—he quit his prestigious job in protest! He invented a whole new way of thinking about laws called "Critical Race Theory," which helps people understand how racism can be hidden in rules and systems. Through books, teaching, and brave actions, Derrick Bell spent his life fighting to make America live up to its promise of justice for all people.

title: The Guitar Master of Taiwan

His fingers could fly across the strings, creating music that made people's hearts soar. But Lu wasn't content just being a star performer—he wanted to share his knowledge with others. He became a beloved teacher who trained generations of guitarists and wrote books about guitar playing that musicians still study today. For nearly seven decades, Lu Chao-Hsuan filled Taiwan with beautiful music until his death in 2017, leaving behind recordings and students who continue his musical legacy.

title: The Late-Blooming Movie Star

She was nominated for an Oscar (one of the biggest movie awards) and suddenly became a Hollywood star in her 80s! Since then, June has appeared in dozens of movies and TV shows, bringing her feisty energy and perfect comic timing to every role. title: The Poet Whose Words Became Weapons

Paektu," compared the country's leader to a mighty mountain and was memorized by schoolchildren across North Korea. Sadly, Cho's life was cut short when he died during the war in 1951, but his poems lived on. Even today, almost 70 years later, North Korean students still learn his poetry! title: The Unlucky King with a Super-Sized Jaw
opener: What if you were born to be king of the world's most powerful empire, but your family tree was so tangled that it gave you serious health problems and the nickname 'the Bewitched'?
story: Poor Charles II of Spain had one of history's most unfortunate royal lives! Despite being ruler of the mighty Spanish Empire—which controlled lands from Europe to the Americas—Charles could barely walk without help and suffered from constant illnesses. Court rumors claimed he was under a witch's spell (earning him the nickname 'El Hechizado'—the Bewitched). The sickly king never managed to have children, and when he died in 1700 at just 38 years old, his death triggered a massive war over who would inherit his throne. Charles was the last of the powerful Habsburg dynasty that had ruled Spain for nearly 200 years!

title: The Doctor Who Proved Blood Moves
opener: What if your experiments with goose quills and pig bladders helped solve one of the biggest mysteries about how our bodies work?
story: George Ent was a scientific detective in a time when people still didn't understand how our bodies worked! Ent performed clever experiments and defended his friend William Harvey, who first proposed the theory of blood circulation. Ent's work was so impressive that he was knighted by King Charles II and became a founding member of the Royal Society—the world's oldest scientific organization that still exists today! Without pioneers like George Ent, we might not understand something as basic as how our own hearts work.

title: Philip the Peacemaker: The Margrave Who Chose Books Over Battles
opener: What if you were born into a family of powerful rulers during a time of constant wars, but decided that reading books and making peace was way cooler than fighting battles?
story: Philip I, born in 1479, became the Margrave (that's like a duke or governor) of Baden in Germany when he was just a teenager. While other rulers of his time were obsessed with conquering new lands and fighting wars, Philip made the surprising choice to focus on peace and learning instead! He collected one of the largest libraries in Europe, supported universities, and worked hard to keep his lands out of the many wars sweeping through Germany. During a time when a new religious movement called the Protestant Reformation was causing violent conflicts everywhere, Philip managed to keep his territory peaceful by being fair to everyone. His smart leadership helped Baden grow prosperous through trade and education rather than conquest!
activity_question: How did Philip I make Baden successful when other rulers were busy with wars?
activity_choices: He built a massive army that scared everyone
He focused on education, peace, and trade
He discovered gold mines in his territory
He conquered all the neighboring lands
activity_answer: He focused on education, peace, and trade
draw_prompt: Draw Philip I surrounded by his massive collection of books while outside his window you can see peaceful towns and busy marketplaces instead of battlefields!
bonus_fact: Philip was nicknamed 'the Scholar' because he spoke several languages and corresponded with the greatest thinkers of his time, including famous Renaissance scholars!

title: The Boy Who Almost Became King: Edmund Mortimer's Wild Story
opener: What if you were born with a better claim to the throne than the actual king, and spent your whole life caught in a dangerous game of royal power struggles?
story: Edmund Mortimer, born in 1391, wasn't just any noble kid - he was actually the rightful heir to England's throne according to many people! When he was just 8 years old, King Henry IV seized the crown from Richard II, even though Edmund had a stronger claim by blood. Instead of becoming king, young Edmund became the most dangerous person in England! He was kept under close watch his whole life, sometimes treated like a prince, other times like a prisoner. When he was 11, his uncle tried to kidnap him to start a rebellion! Later, his brother-in-law used Edmund's royal claim to launch the famous Wars of the Roses. Though Edmund himself remained loyal to the kings who ruled instead of him, his royal blood made his very existence a threat to the crown until his death at age 34.
activity_question: Why was Edmund Mortimer considered dangerous even though he was loyal to the kings?
activity_choices: He had a better claim to the throne by blood
activity_answer: He had a better claim to the throne by blood
draw_prompt: Draw young Edmund Mortimer looking at a crown that seems just out of reach, while castle guards watch him closely from the shadows!
bonus_fact: Edmund's sister Anne married into the powerful York family, and it was through her that the Yorks later claimed the throne, eventually leading to King Edward IV and the infamous Richard III (the king found buried under a parking lot)!

title: The Racket-Wielding Wonder from Poland
opener: What if you were born in the same year as the internet became available to the public, and grew up to become a tennis star representing your country on courts around the world?
story: Born in 1992, Paula Kania-Choduń swung her way into tennis history as one of Poland's talented sports stars. While other kids were just learning to ride bikes, Paula was already developing the powerful backhand that would one day help her compete in Grand Slam tournaments! She turned professional and began representing Poland in international competitions, showing incredible determination as she faced opponents from around the globe. Paula's journey from a young girl with a tennis racket to a professional athlete proves that with practice, passion, and perseverance, you can rally your way to the top!
activity_question: What major tennis tournaments make up the Grand Slam in tennis?
activity_choices: World Cup, Olympics, Pan American Games, Commonwealth Games
Wimbledon, US Open, French Open, Australian Open
Masters Cup, Davis Cup, Fed Cup, Hopman Cup
Monte Carlo Masters, Madrid Open, Italian Open, Shanghai Masters
activity_answer: Wimbledon, US Open, French Open, Australian Open
draw_prompt: Draw Paula Kania-Choduń in action on a tennis court, showing her powerful backhand swing!
bonus_fact: Poland has produced several tennis stars in recent years, including Agnieszka Radwańska and Iga Świątek, who became the first Polish player to win a Grand Slam singles title.

title: K-Pop Superstar: The Rise of Yura
opener: What if you grew up to become a singing and dancing sensation, with millions of fans around the world who knew you by a cool stage name?
story: Born in 1992 in South Korea, Kim Ah-young (better known by her stage name "Yura") danced and sang her way into the spotlight as a member of the popular K-pop girl group Girl's Day. Before becoming famous, she was just a regular kid with big dreams. But after years of intense training—sometimes practicing dance moves for 12 hours a day!—Yura debuted as an idol in 2010. Beyond her singing career, she expanded into acting, appearing in popular Korean TV dramas. Yura's journey shows how dedication, hard work, and following your passions can lead to success in multiple fields!
activity_question: What is the term for Korean pop music that has become popular worldwide?
draw_prompt: Draw Yura performing on stage with colorful lights and an excited audience!
bonus_fact: K-pop idols often train for many years before debuting, learning singing, dancing, languages, and even proper manners for television appearances.

title: The Goalkeeper with Golden Gloves
opener: What if you had such amazing reflexes and quick thinking that you could block balls flying at you faster than a speeding car?
story: Born in 1992 in Germany, Stefan Ortega grew up to become an incredible soccer goalkeeper with lightning-fast reflexes and nerves of steel. As a kid playing in local German fields, Stefan probably never imagined he'd one day be blocking shots from some of the world's greatest strikers! His journey took him through German clubs like Arminia Bielefeld before reaching the English Premier League with Manchester City, one of the world's top teams. Standing between the goalposts, Stefan has made spectacular diving saves that seem to defy gravity, showing that sometimes the most important player on the field isn't the one scoring goals—it's the one preventing them!
activity_question: What is the only player on a soccer team who is allowed to use their hands within the field of play?
draw_prompt: Draw Stefan Ortega making an amazing diving save to block a soccer ball heading for the corner of the goal!
bonus_fact: Professional goalkeepers often have to dive and react in less than half a second to save a shot—that's faster than it takes you to blink your eye!

title: From Small Town to Silver Screen
opener: What if you grew up in a tiny town in Montana but ended up starring in television shows watched by millions of people around the world?
story: Born in 1991 in the small town of Moses Lake, Washington, Pierson Fodé had big dreams that were larger than his hometown. As a teenager, he started modeling and won a competition that opened doors to the entertainment world. But Pierson's real passion was acting! He moved to Los Angeles and landed roles in popular TV shows, becoming most famous for playing Thomas Forrester on the long-running soap opera "The Bold and the Beautiful." Standing tall at 6'2" with a determined spirit, Pierson proved that with talent, hard work, and courage to leave your comfort zone, you can transform from a small-town kid to a star shining on screens across America!
activity_question: What is a "soap opera" called that because:
activity_choices: The stories are so dramatic they make people cry
They originally had soap companies as sponsors
The actors had to wash their hands before performances
The first one was about a soap factory
activity_answer: They originally had soap companies as sponsors
draw_prompt: Draw Pierson Fodé with a script in hand, standing under bright studio lights on a television set!
bonus_fact: Some soap operas have been running for over 50 years, with thousands of episodes—making them some of the longest storytelling projects in human history!

title: Basketball Champion with the Magic Touch
opener: What if you had such amazing basketball skills that you helped win a national championship for one of America's most famous college teams before you were even 21 years old?
story: Born in 1991, Doron Lamb grew up bouncing basketballs on the courts of Queens, New York, where he developed lightning-quick moves and an incredible shooting touch. His talent took him to the University of Kentucky Wildcats, one of college basketball's most legendary teams. In 2012, Doron became a hero when he scored 22 points in the national championship game, helping Kentucky win their eighth NCAA title! His shooting was so accurate that fans called him a "marksman" on the court. After college, Doron played professionally in the NBA for the Milwaukee Bucks and Orlando Magic. His journey from New York playgrounds to basketball glory shows how practice, determination, and performing under pressure can lead to championship moments!
activity_question: How many points is a standard basket worth in basketball?
draw_prompt: Draw Doron Lamb shooting a perfect basketball shot with defenders trying to block him!
bonus_fact: In the 2012 NCAA championship game that Doron Lamb helped win, he made 7 out of 12 shots, including 3 three-pointers, showing why he was known for his shooting accuracy!

title: The Boxing Boy Wonder
opener: What if you were so talented at boxing that by the time you were 21, you had already become a world champion in three different weight classes?
story: Tony Canzoneri wasn't just any boxer - he was a superstar of the ring during the Great Depression! Born in 1908, this Italian-American fighter stood only 5'4" tall but packed incredible power in his punches. Known for his lightning-fast hands and amazing footwork, Tony became one of the first boxers ever to hold world championships in three different weight divisions at the same time! He fought an incredible 175 professional matches during his career, entertaining crowds across America when people really needed something to cheer about. Even though boxing was a tough sport, Tony was known for being a gentleman outside the ring - proving you can be fierce and kind at the same time!
activity_question: In how many different weight divisions did Tony Canzoneri hold world championships at the same time?
draw_prompt: Draw Tony Canzoneri in his boxing stance with three championship belts around his waist, despite being only 5'4" tall!
bonus_fact: Tony Canzoneri was so popular that during the height of the Great Depression, his fights still drew huge crowds who would save up their limited money just to see him box.

title: The Voice That Made Radio Famous
opener: What if your voice was so amazing that you helped create an entirely new way for people to be entertained before television was invented?
story: Back in 1900, a baby girl named Ida Lou Anderson was born who would grow up to change how America listened to the radio forever! In a time when women weren't always encouraged to speak up, Ida Lou became one of the first female professors of public speaking and used her incredible knowledge of voice and presentation to pioneer radio broadcasting. She coached famous radio personalities, including Edward R. Murrow, teaching them exactly how to speak so listeners could really feel what they were saying. Her special techniques for speaking on the radio - like how to pause dramatically or emphasize important words - are still used by broadcasters today! Ida Lou helped transform radio from just a technical gadget into a magical storytelling device that could transport listeners anywhere in the world.
activity_question: What job did Ida Lou Anderson have before becoming a radio pioneer?
draw_prompt: Draw Ida Lou Anderson teaching someone how to speak into an old-fashioned radio microphone, with sound waves visibly flowing from their voice!
bonus_fact: Ida Lou Anderson developed many of her broadcasting techniques while overcoming personal challenges - she used crutches due to a childhood illness but never let physical limitations stop her from becoming a communication pioneer.

title: The Cricket Wizard of England
opener: What if you could hit a tiny ball flying at you faster than a car on the highway, using just a wooden bat, and became famous for it across an entire country?
story: Jack O'Connor wasn't just any cricket player - he was a batting sensation who made crowds gasp in amazement! Born in 1897 in England, Jack developed incredible hand-eye coordination that let him smack cricket balls to all corners of the field. What made him special was his ability to play for hours and hours without getting tired or making mistakes. In cricket, that's called a "long innings" - and Jack was a master at it! He played for Kent County Cricket Club and became legendary for his batting skills during a career that lasted an amazing 30 years. Imagine playing the same sport from when you're a teenager until you're older than most people's grandparents! Through two World Wars and many changes in his country, Jack remained one of England's most beloved sports heroes.
activity_question: How long did Jack O'Connor's cricket career last?
draw_prompt: Draw Jack O'Connor in his cricket uniform, hitting a ball with his bat while fans from different decades cheer him on!
bonus_fact: Cricket matches during Jack O'Connor's era could last up to five days, and players wore all white uniforms that they had to keep clean despite diving and sliding on grass fields!

title: The Big Train: Baseball's Fastest Pitcher
opener: What if you could throw a ball so fast that people called you "The Big Train" because your pitches reminded them of a speeding locomotive?
story: Walter Johnson was the most feared pitcher in baseball history! Born in 1887 on a farm in Kansas, Johnson grew up with strong arms from doing chores and discovered he could throw a baseball faster than anyone had ever seen. His fastball was so blazing quick that batters said it looked like just a white blur zooming past them! During his 21-year career with the Washington Senators, the gentle giant set records that seemed impossible - including 417 wins and 110 shutouts (games where the other team couldn't score a single run). What made Walter special wasn't just his incredible arm, but his kind heart. Unlike many fierce competitors, he refused to throw at batters' heads and was known as one of the nicest guys in sports. When he was finally elected to the very first Baseball Hall of Fame class, everyone agreed: no one deserved it more than The Big Train!
activity_question: What nickname was Walter Johnson given because of his incredibly fast pitches?
draw_prompt: Draw Walter Johnson pitching a baseball with steam coming off it like a train, while batters look terrified at the speed!
bonus_fact: Walter Johnson's pitching motion was unusually smooth and sidearm, rather than overhand like most pitchers. This unique style, combined with his incredible speed, made his pitches even harder for batters to see coming!

title: The Fearless Hero from Two Countries
opener: What if you traveled halfway around the world to a new country, and then became so brave during a terrible war that you were given your new home's highest award for courage?
story: Martin O'Meara was an ordinary Irish immigrant who became an extraordinary hero! Born in Ireland in 1885, Martin moved to Australia seeking adventure and a better life. When World War I began, he joined the Australian Army even though he wasn't required to fight for his adopted country. During the horrific Battle of the Somme in 1916, something amazing happened - Martin repeatedly ran into no-man's land (the dangerous area between enemy trenches) under heavy machine gun fire to rescue wounded soldiers! Over several days, he saved the lives of 25-30 men who would have otherwise died on the battlefield. For his incredible bravery, Martin was awarded the Victoria Cross - the highest military honor in Australia and the British Empire. Only 64 Australians received this medal during the entire war, making Martin's courage truly exceptional!
activity_question: What is the name of the highest military honor that Martin O'Meara received for his bravery?
draw_prompt: Draw Martin O'Meara carrying a wounded soldier to safety while dodging bullets on a World War I battlefield!
bonus_fact: Martin O'Meara was the only Irish-born soldier serving in the Australian forces to receive the Victoria Cross during World War I, making him a hero in two different countries!
